steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
The Rust workload runner under `/app` sometimes leaves its audit stalled or inconsistent with bounded queues, cancelled branches, and child work mixed. Fix it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/executor-audit.json` for the shipped cases from the current inputs.

The JSON has a top-level cases array for amber, cobalt, drake, ember, flint, and graphite. Each case reports name, ok, finished, pending, trace, ledger, and journal. Trace is the accepted work records in acceptance order, where lower accepted turn comes first, lanes are ordered by name within a turn, and ready work within a lane keeps plan-file order. Every record carries case, task, parent, depth, lane, and turn; turn is when that record is accepted. Ledger points to a JSONL file containing the same records in the same order. Journal points to a text file with whitespace-delimited rows in the same order: case task parent depth lane turn.

A clean audit has every case finished, has no pending records, has ok true, preserves parent/depth relationships for child work, keeps cancelled branches and their descendants out of the accepted trace, and leaves the JSON, ledger, and journal in agreement. Child rows keep parent and depth on accepted records for hierarchy only; they do not wait for the named parent to be accepted first. Each row becomes eligible on its own arrival turn, and CAP, lane order, and plan-file order decide acceptance from there. Removing a branch drops that name and every descendant linked through the child chain, not only rows with an identical label. A CAP row limits how many ready records each lane may accept in one turn; overflow remains live for later turns and is considered alongside work that becomes ready later. The single-case helper at `/app/scripts/run-one.sh` must obey the same contract for one shipped case as the matrix run does for all cases. Repeated logical child entries should appear once in the accepted trace, and two fresh matrix runs from unchanged inputs should parse to equivalent audit data.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
root@88e50440921f:/app#

Analysis: We are at a shell prompt in /app with no prior command output. We need to reproduce the failure by running the matrix script, inspect the produced (or missing) outputs, then locate and fix the Rust runner logic that produces executor-audit.json/ledger/journal. First step is to run the shipped scripts and capture errors, then inspect repository structure and relevant source files.
Plan: 1) Inspect /app layout to find Cargo project(s), scripts, and existing output dirs. 2) Run bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh to observe the failure mode and what files get written. 3) If it fails, inspect logs/output directory and any partial audit artifacts. 4) Locate the Rust implementation used by the scripts, then iterate: run failing case(s) via run-one.sh, add targeted debug (if needed), fix ordering/CAP/cancellation/child handling per contract, run tests by rerunning matrix until it produces the required audit JSON and companion files.

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

case_name="${1:?case name required}"
out="${2:-/app/output/executor-audit.json}"
work="${3:-/app/output/runtime}"
mkdir -p /app/output
cargo run --quiet --release --bin executor-ctl -- --case "$case_name" --out "$out" --work "$work"

steps.message:
Analysis: run-matrix.sh fails immediately because `cargo` is not found in PATH, so the Rust binary is never built/run and no /app/output/executor-audit.json is produced. This is an environment/entrypoint issue rather than the workload runner logic yet. We need to locate the Rust toolchain (cargo/rustc) if present, source it (e.g., /root/.cargo/env) or install it if missing, then rerun the scripts to reach the actual audit consistency bug.
Plan: 1) Check whether rust/cargo exists anywhere typical (/root/.cargo/bin, /usr/bin, /usr/local/bin). 2) If rustup environment exists, source /root/.cargo/env and retry cargo. 3) If not present, install Rust toolchain (likely via apt) so cargo is available. 4) Once cargo works, rerun run-matrix.sh to reproduce the actual executor audit issue.

steps.message:
Analysis: No Rust toolchain is present in the container (no cargo, no rustc, no rustup env). The scripts assume cargo exists, so we must install Rust tooling to proceed and then we can work on the actual audit logic bug. This environment currently blocks any further progress on generating executor-audit.json.
Plan: Install cargo/rustc using the system package manager (apt). Then confirm cargo is available and rerun the matrix script. If apt is unavailable or lacks packages, fall back to installing rustup via curl. After cargo works, rerun /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh to get into the workload/audit behavior to fix.

steps.message:
Analysis: The container has no network/DNS access (apt cannot resolve deb.debian.org), so we cannot install Rust via apt. Cargo/rustc are not present anywhere obvious. To proceed, we need an offline toolchain already in the image or vendored in the repo; otherwise we cannot build/run the Rust binary and cannot fix behavior by compiling. Next step is to search the filesystem for an existing cargo/rustc binary or prebuilt executor-ctl, and inspect scripts for alternative execution paths.
Plan: 1) Search common locations and the filesystem for cargo/rustc and for a prebuilt `executor-ctl` binary. 2) Inspect Cargo.toml and scripts/clean-room.sh to understand expected runtime. 3) If a prebuilt binary exists, run it directly to reproduce the audit issue; otherwise, we may need to adjust scripts to use the shipped binary or a local toolchain path.

We should inspect /usr/local/cargo/bin for cargo and add it to PATH for the session (or adjust scripts). Then we can build/run executor-ctl and proceed to the actual audit logic issue.
Plan: 1) List /usr/local/cargo/bin to confirm cargo exists alongside rustc. 2) Temporarily export PATH to include /usr/local/cargo/bin and verify cargo/rustc versions. 3) Run the matrix script with PATH set so it can invoke cargo. 4) Inspect generated /app/output artifacts and then focus on logic fixes if the audit is wrong/stalled.

steps.message:
Analysis: With PATH fixed, run-matrix.sh now completes and writes /app/output/executor-audit.json plus a runtime directory. However, jq is not installed so we couldn't quickly inspect JSON. Next we need to validate the audit contract: cases array contains the 6 shipped cases; each case ok=true, finished=true, pending empty; trace ordering rules; ledger/journal files exist and match trace. We'll inspect JSON via python and check referenced ledger/journal files.
Plan: 1) Use python to parse and summarize /app/output/executor-audit.json (since jq missing). 2) Verify cases present and status fields. 3) For each case, check ledger and journal files exist, read them, and compare records/order against trace. 4) If mismatches/pending/stalls exist, identify which case fails and then inspect relevant Rust code to fix acceptance ordering/CAP/cancellation/child/dedup rules.

arguments.keystrokes:
python3 - <<'PY'
import json, pathlib
p=pathlib.Path('/app/output/executor-audit.json')
print('exists',p.exists(),'size',p.stat().st_size)
a=json.loads(p.read_text())
print('top keys',sorted(a.keys()))
cases=a.get('cases',[])
print('cases',len(cases),[c.get('name') for c in cases])
for c in cases:
    print(c['name'],'ok',c.get('ok'),'finished',c.get('finished'),'pending',len(c.get('pending',[])),'trace',len(c.get('trace',[])))
PY

python3 - <<'PY'
import json, pathlib
from itertools import zip_longest
root=pathlib.Path('/app')
a=json.loads((root/'output/executor-audit.json').read_text())

def norm_rec(r):
    return {k:r.get(k) for k in ['case','task','parent','depth','lane','turn']}

bad=False
for c in a['cases']:
    name=c['name']
    ledger_path=root/c['ledger'] if not str(c['ledger']).startswith('/') else pathlib.Path(c['ledger'])
    journal_path=root/c['journal'] if not str(c['journal']).startswith('/') else pathlib.Path(c['journal'])
    if not ledger_path.exists():
        print('MISSING ledger',name,ledger_path); bad=True; continue
    if not journal_path.exists():
        print('MISSING journal',name,journal_path); bad=True; continue
    # load ledger jsonl
    ledger=[]
    for line in ledger_path.read_text().splitlines():
        line=line.strip()
        if not line: continue
        ledger.append(json.loads(line))
    trace=[norm_rec(r) for r in c.get('trace',[])]
    ledger_n=[norm_rec(r) for r in ledger]
    if trace!=ledger_n:
        print('TRACE!=LEDGER',name,'trace',len(trace),'ledger',len(ledger_n))
        for i,(t,l) in enumerate(zip_longest(trace,ledger_n)):
            if t!=l:
                print(' first diff at',i,'trace',t,'ledger',l)
                break
        bad=True
    # load journal rows
    journal=[]
    for line in journal_path.read_text().splitlines():
        line=line.strip()
        if not line: continue
        parts=line.split()
        if len(parts)!=6:
            print('bad journal row',name,line); bad=True; continue
        case,task,parent,depth,lane,turn=parts
        journal.append({'case':case,'task':task,'parent':None if parent=='-' else parent,'depth':int(depth),'lane':lane,'turn':int(turn)})
    if trace!=journal:
        print('TRACE!=JOURNAL',name,'trace',len(trace),'journal',len(journal))
        for i,(t,j) in enumerate(zip_longest(trace,journal)):
            if t!=j:
                print(' first diff at',i,'trace',t,'journal',j)
                break
        bad=True

print('OK' if not bad else 'FAIL')
PY

python3 - <<'PY'
import json

want=['amber','cobalt','drake','ember','flint','graphite']
have=[c['name'] for c in a.get('cases',[])]
print('want',want)
print('have',have)
print('missing',[x for x in want if x not in have])
print('extra',[x for x in have if x not in want])
PY

steps.message:
Analysis: The audit JSON is produced, but it violates the contract: 3 cases are not finished/ok and have pending records (cobalt/ember/graphite). Also, for every case the journal does not match trace: trace parent field uses "-" string while journal uses None for '-' and also journal ordering/content appears different (first journal row is not the first trace row). That suggests (a) trace records may be using parent '-' instead of null, and (b) journal writing is not using the accepted trace order (maybe writing arrivals or per-lane order) and/or writing a different set (possibly pending). We need to inspect source code for audit emission and acceptance loop to fix: ensure run completes (no stalled pending), cancelled branches removed with descendants, CAP/turn selection correct, dedup of logical child entries, and ensure trace/ledger/journal are the same ordered accepted records with parent=null for root.
Plan: 1) Open src/main.rs and library modules to locate executor loop and audit writer. 2) Inspect types for Record/Audit and how parent is represented/serialized and written to journal. 3) Identify why some cases stall (pending remains) and fix acceptance algorithm per turn, lane order, CAP overflow handling, cancellation/descendant removal, and dedup. 4) Fix journal writer to emit exactly the accepted trace order and use '-' only in journal text (not in JSON), while JSON uses null for no parent. 5) Rebuild and rerun run-matrix.sh, then re-run python validation checks.

Analysis: Record.parent_name is a String and JSON serializer always emits it as a string; roots use "-" which violates the contract (should be null in JSON; '-' only in journal text). Also grep tool (rg) is missing, so we must inspect modules manually. The journal mismatch likely comes from journal writer using a different source/order than trace (maybe pending/arrival order). We also still have unfinished cases with pending work, so the scheduler/acceptance loop is incorrect. Next: inspect drive_g/flow/gate/core for acceptance logic and ledger/journal emission, then fix Record to use Option<String> parent or special-case JSON writing; and ensure journal/ledger write from trace.
Plan: 1) Open drive_g/drive.rs (top-level driver) to see how it runs each case and writes ledger/journal. 2) Inspect flow_e/flow.rs and gate_c/gate.rs for turn-based acceptance, CAP per lane, ordering, cancellation, and dedup rules. 3) Inspect core_a/core.rs and spill_i/mirror/note for any queue/state management causing pending/stalls. 4) After identifying bugs, implement fixes: parent Option in Record + proper serde, correct accepted trace ordering and use it for ledger/journal, correct loop termination so pending drains when possible, and proper drop-cascade for cancellations. Then rebuild and rerun matrix and revalidate via python.

Analysis: Found concrete bugs:
1) Cancellation handling is wrong: gate_c::mark_c only drops exact ident, but spec requires dropping the entire descendant chain. drive_g builds Item.chain but never uses it for removal.
2) CAP/acceptance algorithm is wrong: book_b::fold_b groups by (lane, base_turn) and takes cap per bucket, but CAP is per lane per turn across all ready items, and overflow should remain for later turns and compete with later arrivals. Current logic effectively drops overflow permanently -> pending remains -> unfinished.
3) Ordering is wrong: out sorting is lane then turn then task, but spec says order by accepted turn asc, then lane name asc within turn, then plan-file order (seq) within lane/turn (ready work). Also accepted turn should be when accepted, not base_turn.
4) Journal writer reverses the trace (iter().rev()), causing mismatch.
5) Root parent is stored as "-" string and serialized as string in JSON; contract requires null for no parent (journal can use '-' placeholder).
We need to implement a real turn-based acceptance simulation: for each turn from min_at..max_at while there are ready items or future arrivals, maintain per-lane queue ordered by seq among items with base_turn<=turn and not cancelled; accept up to cap per lane each turn; accepted items get turn=turn; output ordered by (turn, lane, seq). Also dedup repeated logical child entries (same task ident) should only appear once (likely keep earliest seq/at). And cancellation should remove ident and all descendants via chain links.
Plan: Implement fixes in Rust:
1) Change Record.parent_name to Option<String> (and R0 parent_name too) so JSON can emit null for roots.
2) Fix serde_f::row_json to output parent:null when None.
3) Fix note_e to write rows in forward accepted order and print '-' when parent is None.
4) Replace book_b::fold_b with a proper scheduler that:
   - groups rows by lane;
   - iterates turns in ascending order;
   - at each turn, adds newly-ready items (base_turn==turn) into lane queues sorted by seq;
   - accepts up to cap from each lane queue each turn; accepted items removed from queue and assigned turn=current turn;
   - preserves overflow for later turns.
   - outputs accepted records sorted by (turn, lane_name, seq) (turn primary).
5) Fix cancellation: build a set of removed idents expanded to include all descendants: if a plan has DROP X, remove any item whose chain contains X (or whose ancestor chain includes X). Use Item.chain already stored in R0.
6) Ensure dedup: if multiple rows share same task_name, keep only the earliest (by base_turn then seq) and ignore others.
Then rebuild and rerun matrix, and re-run python validations.

apply_patch <<'PATCH'
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: types_f/types.rs
@@
 #[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq)]
 pub struct Record {
     pub case_name: String,
     pub task_name: String,
-    pub parent_name: String,
+    pub parent_name: Option<String>,
     pub depth: usize,
     pub lane_name: String,
     pub turn: u64,
 }
*** End Patch
PATCH

apply_patch <<'PATCH'
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: core_a/core.rs
@@
 pub struct R0 {
     pub case_name: String,
     pub task_name: String,
-    pub parent_name: String,
+    pub parent_name: Option<String>,
     pub depth: usize,
     pub lane_name: String,
     pub base_turn: u64,
     pub turn: u64,
     pub seq: usize,
     pub chain: Vec<String>,
 }
@@
 impl R0 {
     pub fn from_item(case_name: &str, item: &Item) -> Self {
         Self {
             case_name: case_name.to_string(),
             task_name: item.ident.clone(),
-            parent_name: item.parent.clone(),
+            parent_name: (item.parent != "-").then(|| item.parent.clone()),
             depth: item.depth,
             lane_name: item.lane.clone(),
             base_turn: item.at,
             turn: item.at,
             seq: item.seq,
             chain: item.chain.clone(),
         }
     }
@@
     pub fn pack_a(&self) -> Record {
         Record {
             case_name: self.case_name.clone(),
             task_name: self.task_name.clone(),
             parent_name: self.parent_name.clone(),
             depth: self.depth,
             lane_name: self.lane_name.clone(),
             turn: self.turn,
         }
     }
 }
*** End Patch
PATCH

apply_patch <<'PATCH'
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: types_f/serde.rs
@@
 pub fn row_json(row: &Record) -> String {
+    let parent = match &row.parent_name {
+        Some(p) => format!("\"{}\"", esc(p)),
+        None => "null".to_string(),
+    };
     format!(
-        "{{\"case\":\"{}\",\"task\":\"{}\",\"parent\":\"{}\",\"depth\":{},\"lane\":\"{}\",\"turn\":{}}}",
+        "{{\"case\":\"{}\",\"task\":\"{}\",\"parent\":{},\"depth\":{},\"lane\":\"{}\",\"turn\":{}}}",
         esc(&row.case_name),
         esc(&row.task_name),
-        esc(&row.parent_name),
+        parent,
         row.depth,
         esc(&row.lane_name),
         row.turn
     )
 }
*** End Patch
PATCH

python3 - <<'PY'
import json, pathlib
from itertools import zip_longest
root=pathlib.Path('/app')
a=json.loads((root/'output/executor-audit.json').read_text())

def norm_rec(r):
    return {k:r.get(k) for k in ['case','task','parent','depth','lane','turn']}

bad=False
for c in a['cases']:
    name=c['name']
    ledger_path=pathlib.Path(c['ledger'])
    journal_path=pathlib.Path(c['journal'])
    ledger=[]
    for line in ledger_path.read_text().splitlines():
        if line.strip():
            ledger.append(json.loads(line))
    trace=[norm_rec(r) for r in c['trace']]
    ledger_n=[norm_rec(r) for r in ledger]
    if trace!=ledger_n:
        print('TRACE!=LEDGER',name); bad=True
    journal=[]
    for line in journal_path.read_text().splitlines():
        parts=line.split()
        case,task,parent,depth,lane,turn=parts
        journal.append({'case':case,'task':task,'parent':None if parent=='-' else parent,'depth':int(depth),'lane':lane,'turn':int(turn)})
    if trace!=journal:
        print('TRACE!=JOURNAL',name); bad=True
print('OK' if not bad else 'FAIL')
PY

steps.message:
Analysis: The attempted code changes did not apply because `apply_patch` is not available in this environment. Therefore the build/matrix run used the old code, reproducing the same failures (unfinished cases, journal mismatch). We need to edit files using available tools (e.g., heredoc with cat > file, or use sed/perl) to implement the fixes, then rebuild and rerun. First, verify what editors/tools exist (e.g., perl, sed, ed) and then rewrite the affected Rust source files directly.
Plan: 1) Check availability of editing tools (perl, sed, ed, tee). 2) Rewrite the affected Rust files by overwriting them with corrected content using cat <<'EOF' > file. Files to update: types_f/types.rs, core_a/core.rs, types_f/serde.rs, flow_e/flow.rs, book_b/book.rs, drive_g/drive.rs. 3) Run cargo fmt if available (optional) and rebuild. 4) Rerun run-matrix.sh and validate with python that all cases are ok/finished with no pending and that trace/ledger/journal match and parent is null in JSON for roots.

arguments.keystrokes: command -v perl sed ed tee cat >/dev/null && echo "edit tools ok"

cat > /app/types_f/types.rs <<'EOF'
#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
pub enum Entry {
    Root { ident: String, lane: String, at: u64, seq: usize },
    Child { from: String, ident: String, lane: String, at: u64, seq: usize },
    Drop { ident: String },
}

#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
pub struct Plan {
    pub name: String,
    pub cap: usize,
    pub entries: Vec<Entry>,
}

#[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq)]
pub struct Item {
    pub ident: String,
    pub parent: String,
    pub lane: String,
    pub at: u64,
    pub seq: usize,
    pub depth: usize,
    pub chain: Vec<String>,
}

#[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq)]
pub struct Record {
    pub case_name: String,
    pub task_name: String,
    pub parent_name: Option<String>,
    pub depth: usize,
    pub lane_name: String,
    pub turn: u64,
}

#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
pub struct CaseReport {
    pub name: String,
    pub ok: bool,
    pub finished: bool,
    pub pending: Vec<String>,
    pub trace: Vec<Record>,
    pub ledger: String,
    pub journal: String,
}
EOF

Analysis: Edits were successfully applied by overwriting files with cat heredocs. After rebuilding and rerunning run-matrix.sh, all 6 shipped cases are now ok=true, finished=true, and have no pending. The python validation confirms trace, ledger, and journal match exactly for every case (same records and order), and parent handling is consistent (JSON parent null for roots; journal uses '-' which is converted back to None for comparison). Remaining work: ensure run-one.sh also works under same PATH constraint and that /app/output/executor-audit.json exists (it does). We should run run-one.sh for a couple cases and confirm it writes a single-case report correctly.
Plan: 1) Run run-one.sh for a representative previously-failing case (cobalt) into a temporary output file and validate it contains exactly one case and is ok/finished with matching ledger/journal. 2) Re-run run-matrix.sh again to ensure determinism (equivalent audit data) and that it still completes. 3) Confirm final required file /app/output/executor-audit.json exists after matrix run (already does) and paths referenced exist.

arguments.keystrokes:
python3 - <<'PY'
import json, pathlib
p=pathlib.Path('/app/output/one-cobalt.json')
a=json.loads(p.read_text())
print('cases',len(a['cases']))
c=a['cases'][0]
print(c['name'],c['ok'],c['finished'],len(c['pending']),len(c['trace']))
# verify ledger/journal agreement
root=pathlib.Path('/app')

def norm(r):
    return {k:r.get(k) for k in ['case','task','parent','depth','lane','turn']}
trace=[norm(r) for r in c['trace']]
ledger=[norm(json.loads(l)) for l in pathlib.Path(c['ledger']).read_text().splitlines() if l.strip()]
journal=[]
for line in pathlib.Path(c['journal']).read_text().splitlines():
    if not line.strip():
        continue
    case,task,parent,depth,lane,turn=line.split()
    journal.append({'case':case,'task':task,'parent':None if parent=='-' else parent,'depth':int(depth),'lane':lane,'turn':int(turn)})
print('trace==ledger',trace==ledger,'trace==journal',trace==journal)
PY

steps.message:
Analysis: Matrix and single-case runs both complete successfully (with PATH including /usr/local/cargo/bin). The required /app/output/executor-audit.json is produced by run-matrix.sh. All six cases are present and now report ok=true, finished=true, pending empty. Ledger and journal files exist and match the trace order/content. Root parent is encoded as null in JSON (and '-' in journal), and journal is no longer reversed. Previously-stalling cases (cobalt/ember/graphite) now drain pending via correct per-turn CAP handling and cancellation cascades.
Plan: No further commands needed; the required scripts now complete and produce consistent audit outputs for the shipped cases.
